title: Lab 12.4: Single Revision
summary: The planner revises the proposal once, preserving the latency goal while deferring the trust-sensitive decision to the authoritative layer.
decision_question: How much negotiation should the system allow before clarity collapses?

surfaces.name: edge projection
queries: compare the original and revised assumptions

surfaces.name: cloud authority
queries: show which assumptions are now acceptable

proposal.summary: The revised proposal keeps route hint generation at the edge but leaves final authorization and tenant-sensitive branching in the cloud.
assumptions: edge planning may narrow options without finalizing the trust-sensitive branch
the authoritative path still receives enough context to justify the final route

validation.summary: Authority accepts the revision because the planner preserved the latency objective while respecting trust-zone boundaries.
authority_notes: the decision lifecycle is intentionally bounded to one revision cycle

execution.summary: Approval exists, but execution still waits for the chapter to show explicit resolution as its own step.

trace.summary: The trace now captures both the original and revised proposal plus the approval outcome.
queries: show what changed between proposal and revision
show why the revised plan became acceptable

functions.planning: Planning stays explicit while remaining bounded; it may revise a proposal, but it does not negotiate forever.
functions.validation: Validation closes the loop by approving a revision that fits both objective and policy.
functions.execution: Execution remains separate so approval can be observed before the system binds it to runtime actions.

expectations: Bounded revision is what keeps discoverability from turning into endless negotiation.
The reader should be able to compare original intent, feedback, and revised intent directly.
runtime_decisions: the edge now proposes only the locally justifiable part of the change
the cloud remains the place where sensitive authorization is approved
